edges.description: Anterior horn motor neuron injury causes flaccid limb weakness and areflexia with relative sensory sparing.
Viral-triggered neuroinflammatory injury converges on spinal cord gray matter and lower motor neuron dysfunction.
Contemporary EV-D68 strains can differ in which spinal-cord organoid cell populations they infect and how host transcriptional responses are triggered.
edges.source: Human spinal cord organoid EV-D68 tropism

edges.description: MFSD6-dependent viral entry may support EV-D68 infection of neural cells, but the human AFM causal pathway remains under investigation.
ICAM-5-mediated entry provides a candidate mechanism for preferential neuronal infection and anterior horn motor neuron vulnerability.

evidence.reference_title: Acute Flaccid Myelitis: A Clinical Review.

nodes.description: Acute management is primarily supportive and includes close monitoring for bulbar and respiratory muscle involvement, intensive-care support when needed, pain control, prevention of complications, and multidisciplinary neurologic and infectious-disease evaluation.

evidence.reference_title: Acute flaccid myelitis: cause, diagnosis, and management.

nodes.description: AFM localizes primarily to lower motor neuron cell bodies in the anterior horns of the spinal cord. Injury in this spinal gray-matter compartment produces acute flaccid limb weakness, reduced reflexes, and persistent motor disability.

evidence.reference_title: Acute Flaccid Myelitis: A Clinical Review.
evidence.snippet: anterior horn cells of the spinal cord

evidence.reference_title: Acute Flaccid Myelitis Associated With Enterovirus D68: A Review.

nodes.description: Recent experimental work identifies MFSD6 as a host entry receptor for EV-D68 in respiratory and neural cell contexts. This finding provides a mechanistic host-virus interaction that may explain how a respiratory virus gains access to susceptible neural tissues and suggests a potential experimental antiviral target, though it is not yet an established AFM treatment.

evidence.reference_title: MFSD6 is an entry receptor for enterovirus D68.

nodes.description: Human spinal cord organoid studies show that contemporary EV-D68 strains can infect spinal motor neuron-rich and mixed neural organoids, with strain-specific tropism across neurons, astrocytes, and oligodendrocyte progenitor cells. These models support a direct neural infection component but also suggest that infection alone may not fully explain motor neuron loss, leaving secondary immune-mediated injury as an active mechanistic hypothesis.
nodes.id: Human spinal cord organoid EV-D68 tropism

evidence.reference_title: Contemporary enterovirus-D68 isolates infect human spinal cord organoids
evidence.snippet: contemporary strains, but not a historic strain

evidence.reference_title: Strain-specific tropism and transcriptional responses of enterovirus D68 infection in human spinal cord organoids
evidence.snippet: distinct viral tropism and host transcriptional responses

nodes.description: ICAM-5 is a neuron-specific EV-D68 receptor proposed to explain part of the virus's spinal motor-neuron tropism in AFM. This complements MFSD6-mediated entry by modeling receptor plasticity across neuronal and respiratory contexts rather than a single universal entry route.

evidence.reference_title: Enterovirus D68 receptor usage: from static attachment to dynamic entry.
evidence.snippet: intracellular adhesion molecule-5 (ICAM-5) as a neuron-specific receptor that provides a molecular explanation for neurotropism in AFM.

nodes.description: Respiratory muscle weakness or bulbar dysfunction can require intubation, invasive mechanical ventilation, or other airway and respiratory support.

AFM outbreaks are strongly linked to non-polio enteroviruses. EV-D68 is a respiratory enterovirus that can rarely disseminate beyond the respiratory tract and is the leading candidate driver of recent AFM outbreaks, while EV-A71 is another recognized associated enterovirus.

evidence.reference_title: Acute flaccid myelitis: cause, diagnosis, and management.
Epidemiology of acute flaccid myelitis in children in the Netherlands, 2014 to 2019.

nodes.description: Long-term care relies on early, intensive, and individualized rehabilitation, including physical therapy, occupational therapy, bracing, orthopedic monitoring, and functional support for residual motor deficits.

evidence.reference_title: Acute flaccid myelitis: cause, diagnosis, and management.

evidence.reference_title: Acute flaccid myelitis: cause, diagnosis, and management.

nodes.description: Transcutaneous spinal cord stimulation paired with gait training has early pediatric case-series evidence for feasibility and walking-function improvement after AFM-related incomplete spinal cord injury. This is an investigational rehabilitation adjunct rather than established standard care.
nodes.id: Transcutaneous spinal cord stimulation with gait training

evidence.reference_title: Transcutaneous Spinal Cord Stimulation Enables Recovery of Walking in Children with Acute Flaccid Myelitis
